title: ELC(R) Boundary Map — Definitive Three-Tier Classification
anchor_theorem: F16 Characterization Theorem (F16_Characterization.tex, Theorem 2.1): EL_fin = ELC(R)
zero_finiteness: T01 (F16 Theorem 3.1): every k-node F16 tree has at most 2k+2 zeros on any bounded interval

master_characterization.statement: f is in ELC(R) iff (1) it is elementary in the narrow sense — built from exp, ln, and arithmetic over Q in finitely many steps — (2) it is real-analytic — no kinks, jumps, or non-smooth points — and (3) it has finitely many zeros on every bounded real interval.
master_characterization.note: Conditions (2) and (3) follow from (1); all three are stated because each illuminates a different region of the boundary.

inside.reason: Rational combinations of distances and refractive indices; no trigonometry required
Polynomial and rational functions of physical parameters

examples: Von Mises criterion sqrt(sigma1^2 + sigma2^2 - sigma1*sigma2)
inside.reason: Linear, polynomial, or sqrt (= e^(0.5*ln x)) — all in ELC
Exponential, logarithm, and polynomial — fully closed-form; no special functions
Compositions of exp, ln, and rational powers; normalization constants are rational or simple radicals, not Gamma values
Analytic compositions of exp, ln, polynomial; smooth gradient landscape everywhere in domain

examples: Equal temperament f_n = f0 * 2^(n/12) = f0 * exp((n/12)*ln2)
inside.reason: Powers of 2 via exp/ln, logarithm for dB; no trig of real variables required
2^n = e^(n*ln2); Stirling uses exp, ln, sqrt; all in ELC as real functions of a real argument
inside.note: Exact n! for integer n is Tier III-C; Stirling is Tier I

inside.reason: Finite sums of exponentials with real exponents; no trig needed when eigenvalues are real

examples: Snell's law n1*sin(theta1) = n2*sin(theta2) for real theta
boundary_complex.reason: Requires sin/cos of real-valued variables; sin(x) = Im(e^(ix)) is in ELC(C) but not ELC(R); infinite zeros on bounded intervals by T01
cos(omega*t) has zero set {(pi/2 + k*pi)/omega : k in Z}, infinite on any interval of length > pi/omega; excluded by T01 Corollary
boundary_complex.note: The ENVELOPE A*exp(-gamma*t) is Tier I — only the oscillatory factor is excluded

boundary_complex.reason: Time-domain form uses sin/cos; frequency-domain power |P(f)|^2 is Tier I (rational in f)
boundary_complex.note: Frequency-domain energy content is ELC; time-domain waveform is boundary

boundary_complex.reason: cos(pi*t/T) factor is oscillatory over real t; exponential decay eta_t = eta_0*exp(-t/tau) is a Tier I substitute
The sin/cos factor with real argument places these in Tier II; the exponential envelope exp(alpha*t) is Tier I

outside.subtier: A — Non-elementary special functions (violate elementarity; Liouville-Ritt theorem)

entries.reason: Non-elementary; antiderivative of an ELC integrand cannot be expressed as finite exp-log composition for non-integer arguments
Solution to Bessel ODE; no closed exp-log form
Solutions to y'' = x*y; no closed exp-log form
Non-elementary integral of e^t/t
Antiderivative of Gaussian; excluded by Liouville-Ritt (F16 Theorem 3.4)
Polylogarithm; analytic but non-elementary

outside.subtier: B — Piecewise / non-analytic (violate analyticity; F16 Theorem 3.2)

entries.reason: Non-differentiable kink at x=0; F16 Theorem 3.2 excludes all non-analytic functions
Non-analytic kink at x=0
Piecewise switch creates non-analytic join at intersection
Step functions with jump discontinuities at every integer
Jump discontinuity at x=0
Piecewise linear; kink at y*y_hat = 1
Piecewise: quadratic near zero, linear in tails; non-analytic at boundary
Defined on N, not on R; Stirling approximation IS in ELC (Tier I)
Requires integer factorials
Piecewise on integers; no real-analytic extension
Defined on N; takes values in {-1, 0, 1}; not analytic
Multiplicative function on N; integer-valued, not real-domain analytic
Integer step function; non-analytic everywhere
Defined on N; integer-valued

outside.subtier: D — Distributions requiring Gamma at non-integer arguments

entries.function: Gamma distribution PDF f(x; alpha, beta)
entries.reason: Normalization factor Gamma(alpha) is non-elementary for non-integer alpha
Special case of Gamma distribution; Gamma(k/2) normalization excluded
Gamma(a)*Gamma(b)/Gamma(a+b) normalization excluded
Ratio of Gamma functions in normalization
Requires incomplete Beta function or factorials for non-integer parameters
Uncomputable by Rice's theorem
Undecidable
Grows faster than any computable function; non-elementary by any criterion
Partial sums truncated to N terms are in ELC; the limit as a function of x is typically Tier III-A (polylogarithm, etc.)

key_insight: ELC(R) is the unique class of real functions that are simultaneously elementary (built from exp, ln, arithmetic over Q), analytic (no kinks or jumps), and quasi-finitely-zeroed (finitely many zeros on bounded intervals) — and these three conditions are equivalent, with the boundary vivified by three distinct exclusion mechanisms: infinite zero sets (periodic/oscillatory functions, Tier II), Liouville-inexpressibility (special functions, Tier III-A), non-analyticity (piecewise functions, Tier III-B), and discrete domain (number-theoretic functions, Tier III-C).

geometric_venn.ring_2: Analytic functions ⊃ ELC(R) — contains ELC, also sin, cos, Gamma, Bessel, erf
geometric_venn.ring_3: ELC(R) — properly contained in analytic: ELC ⊊ {analytic}
geometric_venn.ring_4: Algebraic functions — partially overlaps ELC: x^(p/q) is in both; e^x is in ELC but not algebraic
key_facts: ELC ∩ {periodic functions} = ∅  (no periodic function is in ELC — key structural fact, by T01)
ELC ∩ {monotone functions} is large but not all monotone functions are ELC (Gamma is monotone for x >= 3/2 but non-elementary)
ELC ∩ {bounded on all of R} = {constants} (any globally bounded ELC function must be constant)
ELC ⊊ ELC(C): adding i as a constant brings in e^(ix) = cos(x) + i*sin(x), whose real/imaginary parts are Tier II

session_sources.X6: Acoustics/music — frequency domain inside, time-domain waveforms boundary
session_sources.X10: Epidemiology — deterministic models inside; gamma distribution boundary
session_sources.X11: Probability distributions — exp-family inside, Gamma-dependent outside
session_sources.X13: Number theory — all discrete functions outside
session_sources.X14: ML losses — smooth losses inside, piecewise losses outside
